did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a hybrid deep learning architecture combining a pre-trained VGG16 convolutional neural network (CNN) for feature extraction and a Capsule Neural Network (CapsNet) for classification to detect melanoma in dermatoscopic images from the ISIC Archive database.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a hybrid deep learning architecture (VGG16 combined with Capsule Neural Network) for melanoma detection. It details a specific methodology including dataset acquisition from the ISIC Archive, model training, and evaluation, reporting new empirical results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) compared to baseline models and literature.

title: Hybrid Capsule Network Architecture Estimation for Melanoma Detection
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning architecture combining a pre-trained VGG16 convolutional neural network for feature extraction and a Capsule Neural Network (CapsNet) for classification to detect melanoma in dermatoscopic images. The model was trained and evaluated using a subset of 4,018 images from the ISIC Archive database, which included 1,084 melanoma cases and 2,934 non-melanoma cases. To address class imbalance, synthetic images were generated for the minority melanoma class, and binary masks were applied to isolate lesion regions. The proposed VGG16-CapsNet model achieved an accuracy of 92.40%, a recall of 90.90%, and a specificity of 92.80%. These results outperformed baseline models using only CapsNet or VGG16 with fully connected layers, as well as several related works in the literature. The authors conclude that combining CNN feature extraction with CapsNet is a promising approach for reliable melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection. The study used 1,084 cases of melanoma from the ISIC Archive database. It is described as a skin cancer characterized by abnormal growth of cells and is considered aggressive and deadly.

The negative class used for binary classification. The study used 2,934 non-melanoma cases (referred to as 'Normal images' in the text) randomly selected from the ISIC Archive to balance the dataset with melanoma cases.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to detect melanoma in dermatoscopic images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma or non-melanoma) using a hybrid VGG16-CapsNet architecture. Evaluation metrics include accuracy (92.40%), recall (90.90%), specificity (92.80%), and AUC (89.60%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A public database of dermatoscopic images provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration. The study acquired a subset of 4,018 images from this archive, including all available melanoma cases and a random selection of non-melanoma cases to balance the dataset. Binary masks provided by ISIC were used to isolate lesion regions.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid VGG16-CapsNet model. Synthetic images were generated for the minority melanoma class to address imbalance.
use.validation: Used for validation during the 50 training epochs to monitor loss and accuracy.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance (accuracy, recall, specificity, AUC).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To distribute samples into training, testing, and validation groups for model evaluation.
To carry out several random tests to guarantee generalization of the model.
To evaluate model stability and performance over 50 training epochs.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, recall, specificity, AUC) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Archive.description: Subset of 4,018 images (1,084 melanoma, 2,934 non-melanoma) with binary masks applied. Synthetic images generated for the minority class to address imbalance.

Baseline and Proposed Models.notes: The VGG16 + CAPS model outperformed the baseline VGG + FCL and solo CAPS models. The paper notes that the proposed hybrid approach reached 90.60% recall in one textual mention (Abstract/Conclusion state 90.90%) and 92.40% accuracy. The model was trained for 50 epochs. The authors state these results outperformed most related works in the literature.

Comparison with Related Works.notes: The proposed VGG16 + CAPS model achieved the best overall accuracy and recall among the compared works, though its AUC (89.60%) was slightly lower than Mahbod et al. [16] (90.69%).

architecture_used.pretraining: VGG16 backbone pre-trained on ImageNet weights

architecture_details.backbone: VGG16 (Convolutional Neural Network) used for feature extraction

capsnet_layers.description: Quantity of capsules equals number of classes (2)

architecture_details.squash_function: Used in final layer to calculate instantiating vectors and class probabilities

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (standard Keras default likely used, but not specified)
training_details.loss_function: Margin loss for CapsNet: Lk = Tk max(0, m+ −||vk ||)2 +λ(1−Tk )max(0, ||vk ||−m− )2, with m+=0.9, m−=0.1
training_details.data_splitting: Holdout method with 5 splits (80% training, 10% validation, 10% testing)
training_details.class_weighting: Weights applied based on sample quantity ratio to address class imbalance
training_details.augmentation: Synthetic images generated for melanoma class using ImageDataGenerator (rotation, mirroring, orientation changes); total dataset size 5509 images

classification_or_output_head.mechanism: Squash softmax function applied to secondary capsule outputs to determine probability of class instantiation

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: VGG16 with Fully Connected Layer (VGG + FCL)
preprocessing_techniques: Application of binary masks provided by the ISIC Archive to isolate lesion regions and exclude non-relevant background areas
Generation of synthetic images for the minority melanoma class using ImageDataGenerator (including rotation, mirroring, and orientation changes) to address class imbalance
Random selection of non-melanoma images to balance the dataset with melanoma cases (targeting a 1:3 ratio)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Synthetic images were generated for the minority melanoma class using the ImageDataGenerator from the Keras library to address class imbalance. The specific geometric transforms applied included rotation, mirroring, and orientation changes. A total of 1,491 synthetic images were generated, bringing the total dataset size to 5,509 images. These augmented images were used exclusively for training.

Class weights were applied during training to handle the remaining imbalance. The weight for the minority melanoma class was calculated by dividing the number of samples in the minority class by the number of samples in the majority non-melanoma class.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid architecture combining a pre-trained VGG16 network for feature extraction and a Capsule Neural Network (CapsNet) for classification proved to be a promising and viable alternative for melanoma detection.
The hybrid VGG16-CapsNet model achieved an accuracy of 92.40%, recall of 90.90%, specificity of 92.80%, and AUC of 89.60% on the ISIC Archive dataset.
The proposed hybrid approach outperformed baseline models using only CapsNet (86.00% accuracy) or VGG16 with fully connected layers (86.20% accuracy).
The results outperformed most related works in the literature in terms of overall accuracy and recall, demonstrating the effectiveness of the method.

The study confirms that combining CNN feature extraction with CapsNet allows the network to better capture spatial relationships and instantiate entities, leading to improved generalization and performance in melanoma pattern identification.

The use of balancing techniques (synthetic image generation and class weighting) was necessary to address the unbalanced nature of the real-world dataset, and the model demonstrated stability during training.
Future work will investigate other CNN architectures for input encoding and evaluate extensions of CapsNet, such as incrementing capsule layers or creating multilevel capsules.
important_remarks: The study uses a subset of the ISIC Archive (4,018 images) rather than the full dataset, which may limit the generalizability of the results compared to studies using the complete archive.
The dataset is artificially balanced by randomly selecting non-melanoma cases to achieve a 1:3 ratio with melanoma cases, which does not reflect the true prevalence of melanoma in clinical practice.
Class imbalance was addressed via synthetic image generation (augmentation) for the minority melanoma class and class weighting, rather than solely relying on the original data distribution.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between the proposed model and baselines or related works.
The model was evaluated using a holdout method with 5 random splits, but specific details on the variance or standard deviation of the results across these splits are not provided.

The proposed hybrid VGG16-CapsNet architecture relies on binary masks provided by ISIC to isolate lesions, meaning performance may be sensitive to the quality and availability of these masks in real-world scenarios.
The optimizer used for training the neural networks is not explicitly reported, which hinders reproducibility.
The study compares results with related works that used different subsets of the ISIC dataset, making direct performance comparisons potentially confounded by dataset composition differences.

The paper is a conference proceeding (IWSSIP 2020) with limited scope and depth compared to journal publications, and future work is suggested to investigate other CNN architectures and CapsNet extensions.
